doc.title: Installment note — equal payments, shifting split
doc.event: On January 1, Osei Co. borrows $100,000 on a 3-year, 8% installment note requiring three equal year-end payments of $38,803. Prepare the entries for the issuance and the first two payments.

variants.label: Equal payments — interest shrinks, principal grows

lines.label: loan proceeds
lines.why: The payment ($38,803) is set so the PV of three of them at 8% equals exactly 100,000 — the note is issued at its principal.
lines.trap: The 'total payments' (3 × 38,803 = 116,409) is NOT the liability — 16,409 of it is future interest that hasn't accrued yet.

lines.label: principal
lines.why: The liability is the principal borrowed, full stop.
lines.trap: Never record the note at the sum of the payments.

lines.label: balance × 8%
lines.why: Interest is always the CURRENT balance × rate: 100,000 × 8% = 8,000 in year 1.
lines.trap: The interest is not payment ÷ something — compute it from the balance, then let principal be the remainder.

lines.label: payment − interest
lines.why: Whatever the fixed payment doesn't spend on interest reduces principal: 38,803 − 8,000 = 30,803. New balance: 69,197.
lines.trap: Principal reduction is the PLUG each year, never a fixed number.

lines.label: fixed payment
lines.why: Same cash every year — that's the installment design.
lines.trap: Fixed cash, moving split. If your interest number repeats next year, you forgot the balance fell.

lines.label: smaller balance, smaller interest
lines.why: 69,197 × 8% = 5,536. The balance fell, so interest fell.
lines.trap: Using year-1's 8,000 again is the installment version of the 'prior period's numbers' error.

lines.label: payment − interest
lines.why: 38,803 − 5,536 = 33,267 of principal. New balance: 35,930 — year 3's payment will clear it (with a small final-year rounding plug).
lines.trap: Watch the drift: interest down, principal up, every single year.

computationPaths.narration: The loop that builds any installment schedule: interest = balance × rate; principal = payment − interest; new balance = balance − principal. Repeat until the balance hits zero (final year plugs rounding). Exact same loop runs your mortgage and your car note.

memorize.body: Installment loop: interest = current balance × rate → principal = payment − interest → new balance = old − principal. Three steps, repeated. The payment itself comes from PV: principal ÷ annuity factor.
Fixed cash, moving split: early payments are interest-heavy, late payments are principal-heavy. (Why early mortgage years feel like renting from the bank.)
Balance-sheet classification trap: the principal due within the NEXT 12 months is a CURRENT liability; only the rest stays long-term. Kieso loves this split.

questions.prompt: A $100,000, 8%, 3-year installment note has equal payments of $38,803. What is YEAR 2 interest expense?

distractors.feedback: 8,000 is YEAR 1's interest (100,000 × 8%). Year 1's payment knocked the balance down to 69,197, so year 2's interest is 69,197 × 8% = 5,536.
The rate applies to the outstanding BALANCE, not to the payment amount.

build.scaffold: Run the loop before touching the entry: balance × rate = interest; payment − interest = principal. The entry then writes itself — expense debit, note debit, cash credit.
